paper.title: Retrieval Trace Scientist for Auto Research Source Recovery

claims.text: The authors have built an agent that converts failed source-recovery traces into a ranked next-query plan and emits a reproducible run packet for an author agent.
claims.rationale: The abstract explicitly states the existence and functionality of the built agent; these are implementation/description claims directly supported by the text.

claims.text: The trace scientist clusters 48 failed source-recovery attempts into 6 recurring failure modes.
claims.rationale: Both the numbered claim and the evidence section restate the same numbers and clustering outcome, directly supporting the claim.

claims.text: The ranked repair plan improves source recovery from 31% to 54% on the saved 48-item fixture.
claims.rationale: The claim is explicitly stated, and detailed replay results (26/48 vs 15/48) provide direct numerical evidence consistent with the percentage improvement.

claims.text: The method has not been tested on live post-deadline news queries.
claims.rationale: The text directly states that such testing has not been done and reiterates this in the evidence section, making this an explicit limitation and indicating further experimentation is needed.

claims.text: The method currently relies on saved trace packets, and live queries may pose challenges such as freshness drift and source churn.
claims.rationale: The limitations section states dependency on saved trace packets and potential issues with live queries, indicating this is a current limitation that would require further work to address.

evidence.text: We build an agent that turns failed source-recovery traces into a ranked next-query plan.
The system clusters failed retrieval attempts, chooses one repair query per cluster, and emits a reproducible run packet for the author agent.
1. The trace scientist clusters 48 failed source-recovery attempts into 6 recurring failure modes.
2. The ranked repair plan improves source recovery from 31% to 54% on the saved 48-item fixture.
3. The method has not been tested on live post-deadline news queries.
The trace audit table lists 48 failed source-recovery attempts and groups them into 6 recurring failure modes.
The replay run recovers 26 of 48 items after ranked repair queries, compared with 15 of 48 items in the baseline run.
The limitation note says live post-deadline news queries have not been tested.
The method currently depends on saved trace packets.
Live queries may introduce freshness drift and source churn.

limitations.text: 3. The method has not been tested on live post-deadline news queries.
The limitation note says live post-deadline news queries have not been tested.
The method currently depends on saved trace packets.
Live queries may introduce freshness drift and source churn.

generated_criticisms.text: The reported improvement from 31% to 54% recovery is only demonstrated on a single 48-item fixture, so it is unclear whether this gain generalizes to other datasets, domains, or retrieval conditions; a broader evaluation would be needed to substantiate the strength and robustness of this performance claim.
generated_criticisms.quote: The replay run recovers 26 of 48 items after ranked repair queries, compared with 15 of 48 items in the baseline run.
generated_criticisms.hidden_assumption: The 48-item fixture is representative of typical usage scenarios and performance gains on this fixture will extrapolate to other tasks and data distributions.

generated_criticisms.text: While the paper states that 48 failed attempts are grouped into 6 recurring failure modes, it does not describe how the clustering is performed or validated, leaving unclear whether these modes are meaningful or stable across runs and datasets.
generated_criticisms.quote: The trace audit table lists 48 failed source-recovery attempts and groups them into 6 recurring failure modes.
generated_criticisms.hidden_assumption: The unspecified clustering procedure yields semantically coherent and reproducible failure modes that would be recognized as such by independent evaluators.

filtered_criticisms.text: The limitation about freshness drift and source churn in live queries is noted but not empirically explored, so the practical impact of these issues on the method’s effectiveness remains unknown.
filtered_criticisms.quote: Live queries may introduce freshness drift and source churn.
filtered_criticisms.hidden_assumption: The challenges introduced by freshness drift and source churn are substantial enough to potentially degrade performance, but could be quantified and mitigated with targeted experiments on live queries.
filtered_criticisms.filter_reason: off_scope: The paper explicitly acknowledges as a limitation that “The method currently depends on saved trace packets. Live queries may introduce freshness drift and source churn,” and also claims that “The method has not been tested on live post-deadline news queries.” The criticism essentially restates that the impact of these issues is unknown and then asks for empirical exploration on live queries. This is precisely an area the authors already mark as untested and out of current scope, so the comment does not add a new check on an unstated assumption; it mainly requests additional experiments beyond the declared evaluation scope and repeats the limitation. Therefore it should be dropped under the given criteria.

selected_next_experiment.hypothesis: A bounded experiment can resolve whether the paper's evidence supports C3: The ranked repair plan improves source recovery from 31% to 54% on the saved 48-item fixture.
selected_next_experiment.baseline: Use the closest reported evidence sentence as the baseline before running the missing check.
selected_next_experiment.keep_condition: claim-specific pass rate reaches the stated target 31% without introducing a new contradiction.
selected_next_experiment.discard_condition: claim-specific pass rate remains below 31% or the run cannot be reproduced.
selected_next_experiment.verify: Run the smallest saved fixture that directly targets C3 and attach the run artifact.
